coredata.dc:title: Valorization of agro-industrial biowaste to green nanomaterials for wastewater treatment: Approaching green chemistry and circular economy principles
coredata.dc:creator: Omran, Basma A.
Baek, Kwang-Hyun
coredata.dc:description: Water pollution is one of the most critical issues worldwide and is a priority in all scientific agendas. Green nanotechnology presents a plethora of promising avenues for wastewater treatment. This review discusses the current trends in the valorization of zero-cost, biodegradable, and readily available agro-industrial biowaste to produce green bio-nanocatalysts and bio-nanosorbents for wastewater treatment. The promising roles of green bio-nanocatalysts and bio-nanosorbents in removing organic and inorganic water contaminants are discussed. The potent antimicrobial activity of bio-derived nanodisinfectants against water-borne pathogenic microbes is reviewed. The bioactive molecules involved in the chelation and tailoring of green synthesized nanomaterials are highlighted along with the mechanisms involved. Furthermore, this review emphasizes how the valorization of agro-industrial biowaste to green nanomaterials for wastewater treatment adheres to the fundamental principles of green chemistry, circular economy, nexus thinking, and zero-waste manufacturing. The potential economic, environmental, and health impacts of valorizing agro-industrial biowaste to green nanomaterials are highlighted. The challenges and future outlooks for the management of agro-industrial biowaste and safe application of green nanomaterials for wastewater treatment are summarized.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Main sustainability criteria for wastewater treatment, including technical, economic, environmental, and social criteria.

Suggested representative scheme showing the preparation process of agro-industrial biowaste extracts (a), flowchart illustrating the green synthesis of nanomaterials via the valorization of agro-industrial biowaste (b). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic showing the general mechanism for the degradation of toxic organic pollutants using nanophotocatalysts.

Illustrated scheme showing the basic mechanism by which TiO2 undergoes photocatalysis. TiO2 acts as a strong oxidizing agent under illumination, lowering the activation energy required for the decomposition of organic and inorganic contaminants. Illumination of the TiO2 surface induces the separation of two types of carriers; an electron (e−) and a photo-induced hole (h+), as well as the reduction of oxygen and the oxidation of water.

Schematic illustration showing the removal of Cr6+ from wastewater by green synthesized Fe3O4 NPs and the regeneration of Fe3O4 NPs using HNO3 and EDTA. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Illustration of the antibacterial mechanism of AgNPs, which includes the adsorption and subsequent penetration of AgNPs into the bacterial cell, causing cell wall deformation, protein denaturation, ROS generation, genomic DNA disruption, phospholipid release, and cytoplasmic leakage.
Some of the main types of secondary metabolites mediating the synthesis of metallic NPs; acids (a), sugars (b), flavonoids (c), alkaloids (d), terpenoids (e), and amino acids (f).

Diﬀerent covalent and non-covalent interactions mediate the preparation of polyphenol-coated NMs, including covalent interactions such as the Baeyer reaction, catechol-boronate interaction, metal chelation, and the Michael addition or Schiﬀ base reaction (a), non-covalent interactions involving hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic and electrostatic interactions (b).
Circular economy, which is primarily based on the “reduce, reuse, recycle, and recover” policy (a), linear economy, based on the take, make, and dispose of policy (b).

Schematic representation showing the food–energy–water nexus approach, which can directly influence the achievement of the 17 SDGs by strengthening synergies, reducing trade-offs, and creating cascading effects between food, energy, and water sectors.
Contaminant removal using green NMs synthesized via the valorization of agro-industrial biowaste extracts.

head.ce:title: Valorization of agro-industrial biowaste to green nanomaterials for wastewater treatment: Approaching green chemistry and circular economy principles

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water pollution is one of the most critical issues worldwide and is a priority in all scientific agendas. Green nanotechnology presents a plethora of promising avenues for wastewater treatment. This review discusses the current trends in the valorization of zero-cost, biodegradable, and readily available agro-industrial biowaste to produce green bio-nanocatalysts and bio-nanosorbents for wastewater treatment. The promising roles of green bio-nanocatalysts and bio-nanosorbents in removing organic and inorganic water contaminants are discussed. The potent antimicrobial activity of bio-derived nanodisinfectants against water-borne pathogenic microbes is reviewed. The bioactive molecules involved in the chelation and tailoring of green synthesized nanomaterials are highlighted along with the mechanisms involved. Furthermore, this review emphasizes how the valorization of agro-industrial biowaste to green nanomaterials for wastewater treatment adheres to the fundamental principles of green chemistry, circular economy, nexus thinking, and zero-waste manufacturing. The potential economic, environmental, and health impacts of valorizing agro-industrial biowaste to green nanomaterials are highlighted. The challenges and future outlooks for the management of agro-industrial biowaste and safe application of green nanomaterials for wastewater treatment are summarized.

ce:section.ce:para: Global water security is one of the most serious environmental issues confronting humanity. The scarcity of fresh and clean water resources, high water consumption, and climate change can lead to crises in the upcoming years (Zarei, 2020). Water contamination occurs as a result of both geological and anthropogenic activities (Deshpande et al., 2020). In many developing countries, effluents are discharged into water bodies without appropriate treatment, posing potential threats to water quality (Bloem et al., 2017). Among the most serious issues of concern are the acute toxicity, carcinogenicity, and mutagenicity resulting from the persistence of organic and inorganic pollutants in water systems (Singh et al., 2020). Wastewater also contains nutrients that can lead to eutrophication, threatening aquatic fauna and flora (Bhatia et al., 2021). Furthermore, the presence of a variety of water-borne pathogenic microbes and undesirable chemical constituents may lead to short- and long-term effects on both the environment and human health, for instance non-infectious biological substances such as endotoxins can cause irritation and allergies when in contact with the skin, or fever, multi-organ failure, and even death if they enter the bloodstream (Fung et al., 2017).

The primary goal of wastewater treatment is to protect downstream users from potential health risks. Wastewater is a significant part of the water cycle and should be carefully monitored throughout the water management cycle, starting with the treatment, distribution, and consumption of freshwater and ending with the collection and treatment of wastewater before its release to the environment. Wastewater treatment is a rational approach to overcome drought and water scarcity problems, particularly in water-stressed regions (Jodar-Abellan et al., 2019). Generally, wastewater remediation can be accomplished via chemical (oxidation, adsorption, flocculation, and disinfection) and physical (screening and flotation) techniques (Lu and Astruc, 2020). Nanomaterials (NMs) are a promising platform for efficacious wastewater treatment as a result of their high surface-to-volume ratio and high reactivity. Furthermore, the development of several nanosorbents, nanocatalysts, and nanomembranes has led to significant advances in wastewater treatment (Nasrollahzadeh et al., 2021). Currently, water remediation is directed towards alternative, green-based processes that align with the principles of green chemistry, sustainability, and circular economy to resolve issues related to the costs and potential risks of secondary pollution. The main sustainability criteria for wastewater treatment are shown in Fig. (1) (Kamali et al., 2019). However, the major bottleneck is the large-scale production of these NMs at economically feasible costs while maintaining their high efficiency, reusability, and recyclability.

Using available, zero-cost, and biodegradable agro-industrial biowaste to fabricate bio-nanosorbents, bio-nanocatalysts, and bio-nanodisinfectants for wastewater treatment offers a promising avenue for environmental protection (Lu and Astruc, 2020). Agro-industrial biowaste residues, generated from agricultural land and industrial processes, account for a significant portion of the municipal solid waste produced, particularly in developing countries (Mihai and Ingrao, 2018; Omran, 2021a, 2021b). Such waste is generally disposed of in landfills as a low-cost disposal option. The landfilling of the millions of tons of agro-industrial biowaste produced annually causes negative environmental impacts, such as gas and leachate generation, air pollution, groundwater pollution, and global warming (Jovanov et al., 2018). Burning agricultural biowaste in farms following the harvest seasons is another traditionally employed method, which leads to the emission of massive volumes of greenhouse gases (e.g., CO2, N2O, and CH4), air contaminants (e.g., CO, NH3, SO2, and volatile organic pollutants), and particulate matters, thereby causing deterioration in air quality and several health problems (Tyagi et al., 2016). Moreover, only a small portion of this biowaste is used for composting and as animal feed (Kee et al., 2021). Green treatment methodologies include the valorization of agro-industrial biowaste to biodiesel (Ng et al., 2020), biochar (Gwenzi et al., 2017) as well as the extraction of various valuable components such as antioxidants, functional lipids, proteins, and carbohydrates (Sagar et al., 2018).

The current linear socio-economic model governs how industries manufacture products and how consumers use and dispose of these products (Parsa et al., 2021). Linear economy follows the “take-make-dispose”, through which raw materials are collected and can be used to manufacture new products before being discarded as waste. However, due to the increasing population worldwide and the availability of potentially beneficial agro-industrial biowaste, the world is rapidly approaching a point where the linear model will no longer be feasible (Barros et al., 2020). Circular economy aims to inculcate the concepts of reduction, reuse, recycling, and recovery of the materials used, and nexus thinking aims at waste reutilization through the interconnections between water, food, and energy resources (Parsa et al., 2021). These principles have garnered increasing attention to achieve sustainability goals (Albrecht et al., 2018). The fundamental principles of circular economy have thus attracted the attention of governments, policymakers, scholars, and public communities as a potential alternative for the current linear economy model. The lack of resources is the primary driving force behind the principles of the circular economy, which, when fully implemented, can result in a significant reduction in the consumption of raw materials and the amounts of waste generated, allowing the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) for water and sanitation, environment and human health to be achieved (Giannetti et al., 2020). Recently, industries have globally begun to shift towards achieving zero-emission goals, which are critical for achieving circularity (Orejuela-Escobar et al., 2021). The agricultural and industrial sectors worldwide significantly contribute to biowaste emissions, and reducing agro-industrial biowaste is a primary criterion that can lead to successful circular practices (Mohan and Katakojwala, 2021).

Unlike previous reviews that focused on the biological synthesis of NMs using various biological entities (e.g., bacteria, fungi, algae, and plants), this review emphasizes the valorization of agro-industrial biowaste to produce bio-nanosorbents, bio-nanocatalysts, and bio-nanodisinfectants for use in wastewater treatment. This review highlights the current trends in using green NMs prepared via the valorization of agro-industrial biowaste for the removal of several toxic water contaminants, including (i) organic pollutants (e.g., dyes, phenols, nitrophenols, chlorinated compounds, pesticides, and pharmaceutical compounds), (ii) inorganic pollutants (e.g., heavy metal ions and nutrient salts that may occasionally be present at high concentrations, posing a threat to the water supply), and (iii) water-borne pathogenic microbes, with particular emphasis on the mode of action. It also describes the most common biomolecules in agro-industrial biowaste that can tailor the fabrication and tune the properties of green synthesized NMs and their mechanisms. The potential benefits and strong correlation between the valorization of agro-industrial biowaste and the fundamental principles of green chemistry, circular economy, nexus thinking, sustainability, and zero-cost and zero-waste manufacturing approaches are proposed. The economic, environmental, and health impacts of valorizing agro-industrial biowaste to produce green NMs and future research directions are discussed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Valorization of agro-industrial biowaste to green NMs
ce:section.ce:para: The agricultural and industrial sectors generate millions of tons of biowaste annually, and its accumulation does not allow the pursuit of circular economy and sustainability principles (Sheldon, 2016). Agricultural waste byproducts such as seeds, peel/skin, pulp, membrane residues, shells, tassels, husks, and cobs are typically discarded and left to rot or incinerated. The recovery/reuse/upcycling of agro-industrial biowaste is rewarding because of its high potential value. Utilizing the biopotential of this waste to produce commercial high-value-added products (can enable the establishment of a closed-loop economy, in which no waste is generated (Kowalska et al., 2017). Several treatment technologies, including mechanical (e.g., pelletization), thermochemical (e.g., pyrolysis, combustion, and biomass gasification), and biochemical (e.g., transesterification, anaerobic digestion, and fermentation) methods have been applied to produce valuable products from bio-wastes (Kapoor et al., 2020). Some of these techniques are listed in Table S1, supplementary materials.

Nanobiotechnology is concerned with the biological tailoring and design of nanoscale materials (Omran, 2020a, 2020b). Biologically fabricated nanostructures have been investigated for medicine, drug delivery, environment, materials science, and energy applications (Mughal et al., 2021). In light of the above, the following section emphasizes the valorization of agro-industrial biowaste to produce green bio-nanocatalysts, bio-nanosorbents, and bio-nanodisinfectants that can be used for wastewater treatment. Agro-industrial biowaste can mediate the synthesis of green NMs using precursor metal salts, such as AgNO3, HAuCl4, etc., which can help reduce the cost and energy requirements when compared with the current physical and chemical synthesis methodologies (Omran et al., 2021a). The use and release of toxic chemicals and byproducts are considerably reduced because the biomolecules present within the biowaste extracts act as naturally occurring reductants, stabilizers, and capping agents (Omran et al., 2021b). Thus, the green synthesis of NMs occurs through environmentally friendly processes. Furthermore, it is facile and relatively reproducible, allowing the production of stable nanostructures (Xu et al., 2019). The biosynthesis of NMs from agro-industrial biowaste is more advantageous than that using other biological sources such as bacteria, yeast, fungi, and algae because elaborate culturing processes or cell maintenance procedures are not required (Abdelbasir et al., 2020). Biogenic NMs are excellent candidates for remediating drainage systems, treatment plants, membrane bioreactors, and other wastewater treatment and purification systems. However, controlling the size, long-term stability, agglomeration, and sedimentation of biogenic NMs during the treatment of effluents remains a persistent challenge for large-scale commercial applications. A representative scheme illustrating the preparation of agro-industrial biowaste extracts, including several steps such as washing, drying, grinding, extraction, filtration, and evaporation, is presented in Fig. 2 a. A flowchart describing the green synthesis of NMs via the valorization of agro-industrial biowaste extracts in the presence of the metal salt precursors is given in Fig. 2b.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Removal of contaminants by green NMs that can act as bio-nanocatalysts, bio-nanosorbents, and bio-nanodisinfectants
ce:section.ce:para: Numerous organic and inorganic hazardous pollutants are discharged in water bodies, causing widespread concerns. Green NMs synthesized via the valorization of agro-industrial biowastes exhibit a promising role in wastewater treatment. According to a recent systematic literature review, the UK and European countries had the highest number of publications investigating the application of the circular economy to the agro-food industry (Esposito et al., 2020). The high percentage could be justified by the European Union's emphasis on the adoption of circular economy models in all sectors through the development of several policies (Petit et al., 2018). Additionally, Italy contributed with a high number of papers, which could be due to Italy's strategic planning on the circular economy, as well as the increased regulatory focus, involving law 221, December 28, 2015 and other legislative decisions (Ghisellini and Ulgiati, 2020). This research area is driven by three major criteria; (i) the environment, (ii) the economics, and (iii) natural resource degradation (Reddy et al., 2017). Table 1 shows some examples of the green synthesized NMs that can be used for the removal of various wastewater contaminants.

Organic contaminants in wastewater include chemically active compounds discharged from industries such as dyes, phenolic compounds, polyaromatic hydrocarbons, surfactants, pesticides, humic substances, and pharmaceutical residues. Organic water contaminants can cause cancer, non-Hodgkin lymphoma, and hormonal imbalance that can disrupt developmental and reproductive processes, leading to damage of kidney and liver, and nervous system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dyes, types, environmental concerns, and their treatment using green synthesized NMs
ce:section.ce:para: Dyes are synthetic organic compounds that are either water- or oil-soluble, whereas pigments are insoluble organic compounds that remain in particulate forms (Vazquez-Ortega et al., 2020). Dyes have color, unlike other organic compounds, because they absorb light in the visible light region (400–700 nm) and contain at least one chromophore (Verma and Samanta, 2018). Dyes usually contain two key components, which are the chromophores and auxochromes. Chromophores are composed of various functional groups, such as alkenes (─C═C─), carbonyl (–C = = O), thiocarbonyl (─C═S), azo (–N = = N–), nitroso (─N═O), or nitro (–NO2) and they are primarily responsible for the dyeing potential. On the other hand, auxochromes contain functional groups that can be acidic, such as sulfonic acid (–SO3H), carboxylic acid (–COOH), and hydroxy (–OH) groups, or basic, such as amino (–NH3), secondary amine (–NHR), and tertiary amine (–NR2). They are responsible for increasing the affinity of fibers for coloration and decreasing water solubility through electron donation or removal (Benkhaya et al., 2020). Dyes have a conjugated system with alternating single and double bonds that exhibit electron resonance and act as a stabilizer in organic compounds (Sharma et al., 2021). Moreover, they are distinguished by their good water solubility, the potential to be absorbed and maintained by fibers, and their stability by being able to withstand washing, dry cleaning, and prolonged light exposure (Teo et al., 2021). Dyes can be classified into several categories based on their chromophores, which can involve anthraquinone (Li et al., 2022), azo (Alsamhary et al., 2021), indigo (Oliveira et al., 2020), nitro (Woo and Jhung, 2021), nitroso (Masoud et al., 2015), triphenylmethane (Yao et al., 2021), cyanin (Liu et al., 2021a), xanthene (Doyle et al., 2021), oxazine (Paudics et al., 2021), and acridine (Gadah and Basaleh, 2020) dyes. Dye effluents released by textile industries are the most polluting effluents among all industries that pollute aquatic systems, posing a wide range of health and environmental risks. They are difficult to biodegrade as a result of their aromatic structure, xenobiotic nature, photostability, and high thermal resistance (Sharma et al., 2021). They affect water transparency and quality, reduce light penetration, increase biochemical oxygen demand, and impair aquatic flora's photosynthetic activities, causing widespread concerns among environmentalists (Sharma et al., 2021). The discharge of dye effluents has a significant impact on algae and aquatic macrophytes, as well as disrupting the integrity of biological systems (Al-Tohamy et al., 2022).

TiO2, as a classical semiconductor, is the most extensively investigated photocatalyst (Fig. 4 ). TiO2-based photocatalysis relies on the release of highly active species such as •OH, O2 •‒, and peroxides (
O 2
2 −
), which can degrade organic contaminants under UV light, visible light, or sunlight (Ge et al., 2021). TiO2 NPs possess unique photocatalytic potency, chemical and biological inertness, and high oxidation capacity. The morphology, crystallinity, hydrophilicity, and band gap energy can be fine-tuned by controlling the type, chain length, and intensity of the template molecules. In one study, sugarcane bagasse (SB) was used to immobilize 6 nm-sized TiO2 NPs synthesized by the sol-gel technique. Under visible light, this bio-nanocatalyst functioned as an organic–inorganic hybrid photocatalyst that could efficiently degrade methyl orange (MO) (Xue et al., 2018). The achieved MO degradation efficiency was 95% within 5 h. The hybridization of TiO2 NPs with cellulose or beta-cyclodextrin (i.e., the organic components in SB) altered the TiO2 electronic structure, leading to an expansion of its absorbance range, separation of photoelectrons and holes, and enhancement of the photocatalytic performance. The photocatalytic activity improved by increasing the amount of SB as a result of the increase in the hybridization degree. However, a further increase in the amount of SB reduced the photocatalytic activity due to a decreased TiO2 content of the prepared photocatalyst. In another study, waste lignocellulosic biomass derived from papaya extract was used to tailor the fabrication of iron oxide (IO) NPs using a chemical precipitation technique (Ahmed and Ahmaruzzaman, 2015). These green synthesized IONPs were subjected to different annealing temperatures (i.e., 353, 573, and 773 K). An increase in the annealing temperature led to the formation of larger particles with improved crystallinity and magnetic properties. IONPs annealed at 773 K exhibited the highest sequestration efficiency of 96.45% for chlorazol black E. The mechanism of sequestration was governed by hydrogen bonding and π–π dispersive interactions.

The use of NMs for wastewater treatment at a large scale requires the resolving of several inherent technical bottlenecks such as agglomeration, difficulties in recovery, leakage into the environment, and the potentially negative effects on humans and ecosystems. One promising approach to overcome these limitations is the synthesis of nanocomposites, which are multi-phased solid materials prepared as porous media, colloids, gels, and copolymers that can reduce NP leaching into the environment while also improving biocompatibility (Hassan et al., 2021). The selection of a suitable host is critical because of the influence the host has on the performance of the resulting nanocomposite. Nanocomposites are significantly more stable, dispersed, and recoverable than free NPs and are the most promising agents for applying and transferring water nanotechnology from the lab to large-scale applications (Ates et al., 2020).

Bananas are the second most cultivated fruits after citrus, accounting for approximately 16% of worldwide fruit production (Acevedo et al., 2021). During processing, the banana industry generates a large amount of waste, which includes pseudostems, leaves, peels, and rachis. These wastes can be used in a variety of applications to achieve the goals of circular economy, including biofuel production (e.g., biogas, biohydrogen, biodiesel, biochar) (Tsapekos et al., 2018; Al-Mohammedawi et al., 2019; Balajii and Niju, 2020; Te et al., 2021), wastewater treatment (Mouiya et al., 2019), bioplastic production (Ai et al., 2021), and fabrication of NMs (Noonari et al., 2019). A hexagonal-shaped cellulose/SiO2 nanocomposite (CSNC; average size 20 nm) was synthesized using waste banana peel extract as a template for the synthesis of silica NPs (Ali, 2018). Using synthetic and real samples, the sorption capacity of CSNC for methylene blue (MB) fitted the Langmuir adsorption isotherm and followed the pseudo-second-order kinetic model. The reaction was exothermic and spontaneous, indicating a chemisorption mode. The calculated adsorption capacities in an aqueous solution and a real water sample were 78.75 and 58.81 mg/g, respectively. A better catalytic activity was attained via the dispersion of NiFe2O4 NPs on cellulose nanofibers (CNFs), which acted as excellent templates for the stabilization and agglomeration prevention of NiFe2O4 NPs (Gupta et al., 2020). The inherent magnetic properties of pristine NiFe2O4 NPs make them agglomerate, leading to low dispersion, poor stability, and a reduction in their specific surface area. CNFs reduced the agglomeration of NiFe2O4 NPs, allowing their well-dispersion, which led to enhanced catalytic performance. The green nanocomposite functioned as a catalyst for the degradation of remazol black 5 during which photo-Fenton degradation was the key process. The effects of H2O2, catalyst, and light source were studied individually. Negligible degradation was noted with the addition of H2O2 only, which could be attributed to the low oxidation potential of peroxide. Moreover, very little degradation was observed with the addition of ferrite only in the presence of light. However, the degradation rate was improved to some extent using ferrite and H2O2 in the absence of light. When H2O2 and ferrite were used in the presence of light, the degradation rate increased dramatically, implying that the three components (i.e., H2O2, light source, and catalyst) were required for fast degradation. When the catalyst was exposed to visible light, an electron-hole pair formed and interacted with the oxidant (H2O2) and OH− (from water), resulting in the formation of •OH, which acted as a highly active species for the degradation of remazol black 5 (Fig. S1), supplementary materials.

Hydrochars are highly chemically stable carbon materials that can be used as soil amendment agents (Ahmad et al., 2020) and adsorbents (Khosravi et al., 2021). They have an aromatic skeleton and several functional groups on their outer layers, making them perfect candidates as photocatalyst supporting materials. In one study, hydrochar was biologically prepared from hazelnut shells and olive residues via hydrothermal carbonization and was then interconnected with bare TiO2 NPs produced via a solvothermal process (Donar et al., 2018). The waste biomass-based hydrochar/TiO2 nanocomposite exhibited superior visible light-assisted photocatalytic performance compared with other carbon-based TiO2 composites, e.g., fullerene and multi-walled carbon nano-tubes (CNTs). The presence of functional groups derived from the hazelnut shells and olive residues, as well as the delocalization of the furanic-conjugated system, contributed to an improved photodegradation potential. However, a potential limitation of this study is that raw materials, such as hazelnut shells and olive residues are not universally available. Carbon dots (CDs) can be used as fluorescent probes in bioimaging, photocatalysis, bio- and chemical sensors, and other applications (Ashra et al., 2020). The facile and chemical-free synthesis of biocompatible spherical-shaped CDs (∼50 nm) via a one-pot carbonization method has been reported using a date palm frond (Kavitha and Kumar, 2018). The bioderived CDs exhibited a photocatalytic potential for MO degradation in the presence of H2O2 under sunlight. Through a series of redox reactions, the H2O2 molecules adsorbed on the CDs photo-decayed into active •OH, which has a high oxidation potential for the photodegradation of MO to CO2 and H2O. Furthermore, strong π–π interactions between the mesoporous CDs and MO might have facilitated the catalytic process. Under sunlight, CDs acted as both electron donors and acceptors, resulting in an excellent light-harvesting potential with the ability for unique photo-induced electron transfer, leading to the photocatalytic decomposition of MO (Zhang et al., 2016a). The term “noble metals” describes transition metals such as Au, Ag, Pt, and Pd with high ionization energy and low oxidation potential due to the small sizes of their atoms (Li and Baek, 2020). The ionization energy and oxidation potential of noble metals change substantially at the nanoscale, allowing for a variety of novel reactions. The most common method for producing noble metal NPs is the reduction of the corresponding metal salts, followed by the use of stabilizers to control nucleation and growth of nanocrystals. Noble metals prevent the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs while inducing surface charge separation (Chen et al., 2021). Bimetallic NPs, formed by combining two different metals, can effectively address some of the drawbacks associated with monometallic NPs such as leaching, decreased recyclability and efficiency over time, and function in a limited pH range. Reproducible, highly stable, and monodispersed bimetallic ZnO/AgNPs were synthesized using pomegranate peel extract as a reductant and stabilizer, which acquired a custard-apple morphological structure with a diameter of 100 nm (Kaviya and Prasad, 2015). The uniform and well-distributed ZnO/AgNPs facilitated charge separation and improved the photocatalytic potential for the degradation of MB under solar irradiation. The addition of peroxymonosulfate (PMS) to ZnO/AgNPs increased the degradation rate from 70% to 95%, with the complete mineralization of MB within 3 h. When combined with ZnO/AgNPs, PMS oxidized rapidly to produce •OH and SO4 •-, which led to an increase in the overall degradation rate. The biogenic ZnO/AgNPs were stable for five cycles, as confirmed by SEM, but the ZnO/AgNP structure collapsed after the fifth cycle.

TiO2 is one of the most studied photocatalysts because of its high UV absorption and refractive index, excellent photoelectric conversion efficacy, good photocatalytic activity, photostability, chemical stability, long-term corrosion resistance, and non-toxicity (Spadavecchia et al., 2014). However, three key drawbacks limit the practical application of TiO2-based materials, including (i) the large band gap, (ii) the limited rate at which photo-oxidation occurs due to the high recombination rate of electron-hole pairs, and (iii) the weak separation efficiency of the photocarriers (Gopinath et al., 2020). Doping is an excellent strategy for addressing these drawbacks, increasing the absorption capacity and photocatalytic activity, and facilitating the engineering of the electronic properties of nanophotocatalysts (Lee et al., 2021). Doping can be categorized into self-doping (Liu et al., 2021b), non-metal doping (Basavarajappa et al., 2020), transition metal doping (Moulavi et al., 2021), and rare-earth metal doping (Prakash et al., 2021). Doped ions provide additional energy levels into the band structure, aid in trapping electrons or holes, and facilitate the separation of carriers from the bands, allowing more carriers to diffuse to the surface. The oxidation state, surface chemistry, and ionic radii of a dopant can improve the efficiency of the doping process, resulting in better catalytic properties. Nephelium lappaceum peel extract was used to synthesize spherical-shaped Ag-doped TiO2 NPs (30–200 nm) via an environmentally friendly procedure (Kumar et al., 2016). The prepared Ag-doped TiO2 NPs exhibited a photocatalytic activity for the degradation of MB in an aqueous solution with a removal efficiency of >80%. AgNPs trapped electrons and promoted interfacial charge transfer processes in the doped-nanocomposite, resulting in a decrease in the recombination of photo-induced electron-hole pairs, and enhancement of the photocatalytic potential of TiO2 NPs (Kodom et al., 2015). Using a green-assisted method, hollow, spherical, and porous Mg-doped ZrO2 NPs were synthesized using Aloe vera gel extract, a colorless gel found in the inner part of fresh A. vera leaves (Renuka et al., 2016). The Mg-doped ZrO2 NPs exhibited high photocatalytic performance with a degradation efficiency of 93%. The increased activity was ascribed to the ratio of monoclinic to tetragonal phases and the number of oxygen vacancies, which improved the charge carrier separation under UV light. The photocatalyst showed a high mineralization potential and recyclability for five successive cycles.

A significant hindrance is the lack of piloted technology on a commercial scale. For instance, renewable energy and nanotechnology products necessitate significant capital investment to compete with their predecessors. To ensure the environmental impact and economic sustainability of valorizing agro-industrial biowastes into value-added products, several aspects should be considered such as the availability of biowastes and tecno-economic potential analysis. For instance, cellulose fibers prepared from biowastes require high-quality control regarding size and shape uniformity (Siripongpreda et al., 2021). One of the most significant constraints for the development of cellulose from biowastes is the purity of the regenerated cellulose. Another significant aspect is the return of investment and the pay-back period, as well as the geographical location of the waste valorization area (Usmani et al., 2021). Another logistical issue is related to the most efficient time for the collection of waste from agricultural farms. The time between storage and distribution was found to be important for the quality of cellulose waste (Bottani et al., 2019). Pilot-scale studies are required to obtain an accurate estimate of the operating costs of a wastewater treatment facility (Younas et al., 2021). Several companies worldwide are becoming increasingly interested in the use of residual biomass. Beta Renewables built the world's first cellulosic ethanol industrial operating plant in 2012. As a result of the success of this initiative, other companies, such as Crescentino in Italy, started developing industrial waste reuse plants in 2015 (Freitas et al., 2021). In addition to the afore-mentioned companies, other companies in India, Brazil, and China have implemented technologies for using cellulosic materials to yield ethanol and other compounds (Rosales-Calderon and Arantes, 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phenols, nitrophenols, chlorinated compounds, and organophosphorus pesticides
ce:section.ce:para: The Fenton reaction was first developed in 1890 by Henry John Horstman Fenton (1894). The Fenton reagent is composed of ferrous ions and an oxidant, H2O2. It produces transitory but remarkably reactive species such as •OH with high oxidizing potential (Jain et al., 2018). Despite being discovered over a century ago, it was not employed to remove hazardous organic contaminants until 1960 (Huang et al., 1993). Fenton reactions can be homogeneous with direct interactions between the Fenton reagent and the compounds targeted for degradation (Sreeja and Sosamony, 2016). Homogenous Fenton reactions have several advantages, including the ability to proceed at room temperature, high reaction rates between H2O2 and Fe (II), use of non-toxic reagents, and potential applications in a variety of contaminated and wastewater treatment plants. However, the main disadvantages of this process include sludge formation after post-treatment processes, activity in a limited pH range, high iron discharge, and difficulties associated with the recovery of iron ions (Jain et al., 2018). Homogeneous Fenton reactions require acidic conditions (i.e., a pH < 4) to allow the conversion of Fe+2 and Fe+3 and to enhance the reaction rate (Tang et al., 2019). When the pH of a solution exceeds 4, ferric hydroxide sludge is produced, a portion of the catalyst is lost, and the reaction efficiency is reduced (Garrido-Ramírez et al., 2010). The process is economically unfeasible because of the required strongly acidic conditions, which necessitates the continuous addition of chemicals during wastewater treatment, and additional costs are incurred for the handling and disposal of solid sludge. Conversely, in heterogeneous Fenton-like reactions, physical and chemical changes arise on the surface-active sites of a catalyst. The degraded molecules are desorbed at the end of the reaction, and the active sites can allow subsequent interactions with other molecules that attach to the surface of the catalyst (Hussain et al., 2021). The processing and economic limitations related to homogeneous Fenton reactions, including the relatively high chemical input, catalyst loss, and massive amounts of generated sludge, can be resolved via heterogeneous Fenton-like reactions (Ribeiro and Nunes, 2021). The low iron emission, environmental friendliness, and easy catalyst separation from the solution are other advantages of heterogeneous Fenton-like reactions. In one study, FeNPs were synthesized using green, black, and oolong tea extracts, and tested as heterogeneous Fenton-like oxidation catalysts for the degradation of monochlorobenzene (Kuang et al., 2013). The degradation efficiencies of monochlorobenzene by FeNPs synthesized using black, oolong, and green tea extracts were 39%, 53%, and 69%, respectively. Green tea extract-mediated FeNPs exhibited the highest degradation efficiency, which could be attributed to their high caffeine and polyphenol contents that acted as reducing and capping agents.

Cobalt silica NPs can act as active heterogeneous catalysts for phenol oxidation in the presence of H2O2 as an oxygen source. Since heterogeneous catalysis is a surface-based reaction, the physical properties of cobalt silica NPs (i.e., pore dimension and pore volume) have a significant role in determining the accessibility of the reactant and governing the transfer of phenol and the oxidant to the pore channels of a bio-derived cobalt silica nanocatalyst. The sol-gel technique was applied in a study to prepare a highly mesoporous cobalt silica catalyst (9.4 nm) derived from rice husk (Andas et al., 2014). The resulting large pore volume (1.21 cc/g) and wide pore diameter (9.5 nm) facilitated the diffusion of phenol and H2O2 into the inner cavities of the cobalt-silica bio-nanocatalyst. Tailoring a well-ordered porous material with parallel and hexagonal arrays provides more active sites for reactant diffusion than disordered materials, which allowed the dispersion of cobalt onto the silica (i.e., support material) surface and increased the diffusion of •OH onto the active sites inside the pore channels of the cobalt-silica bio-nanocatalyst (Faraji et al., 2017). Nitrophenol derivatives are generated during pigment, agrochemical, and pharmaceutical manufacturing; 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) is a toxic bio-refractory phenolic compound that can adversely affect the liver, kidney, and central nervous system (Kalaimurugan et al., 2021). Nanosized alumina (γ-Al2O3) has a high adsorption potential owing to its large surface area and high mechanical strength (Mavrič et al., 2019). By deploying polyphenols from bayberry tannins (BT), a heterogeneous catalyst composed of AuNPs and supported on γ-Al2O3 was prepared via a one-pot reaction (Huang et al., 2011). BT mediated the initial reduction of AuCl4 − to AuNPs and acted as a reductant and stabilizer. A bridged nanostructure (Al2O3/BT/Au NPs) formed via glutaraldehyde-assisted self-crosslinking on porous γ-Al2O3. The well-dispersed spherical-shaped AuNPs (∼23 nm) supported on γ-Al2O3 and assisted by NaBH4 functioned as an active and reusable catalyst for the reduction of 4-NP to 4-AP. The prepared nanostructure triggered the reduction reaction by allowing the flow of electrons from BH4 to the nitrophenol molecules, resulting in the conversion of nitro moieties to amino moieties. Moreover, the catalyst was reusable for four successive cycles.

Adsorbent cost is another significant factor that should be taken into consideration because most of the developing adsorbents contain hybrid materials that involve two or more different materials (Reddy et al., 2017). For instance, carbon NMs such as graphene and carbon nanotubes (i.e., single and multi-walled) have been used to tailor superior composite nanoadsorbents (Gusain et al., 2020; Omran and Baek, 2022). The price of these carbon NMs ranges from $0.10 to $1000 per gram, based on grade, effectiveness, and functionalization (Adeleye et al., 2016). However, to accurately estimate the overall cost of an adsorbent, the costs of raw materials, power, labor, and processing must be considered (Reddy et al., 2017). Post-sorbent-based materials can have a significant role in developing value-added green materials. Regeneration of post-sorbents is a critical stage in making an adsorption technique sustainable. As a result, it is crucial to find innovative routes for using post-sorbents as value-added products. Few studies have addressed the management of spent regenerated adsorbents (Feng et al., 2013; Gómez-Pastora et al., 2014; Reddy and Yun, 2016). The value-added applications of metal-loaded sorbents have been discussed by Dodson et al. (2015) to address the economic and management issues associated with these post-sorbents. Post-sorbents can also be used to synthesize carbon-encapsulated metal NPs (Pyrzynska, 2010), bioactive compounds (de Souza et al., 2011), fertilizers and soil conditioners (Li et al., 2016), catalysts (Wu et al., 2014), and feed additives (Witkowska et al., 2016). However, before using post-sorbents as fertilizers or soil conditioners, the levels of potentially toxic elements in the soil should be identified (Reddy et al., 2017). Dye-loaded adsorbents could be used in the fabrication of value-added biochar or combined with metal additives to manufacture carbonaceous NPs.

Pharmaceutical contaminants can alter the quality and physicochemical properties of water, posing a significant risk to human health and the environment (Santhosh et al., 2016). Bioinspired NMs can be used for the remediation of water polluted with pharmaceutical residues, particularly antibiotics, which were first reported to pollute water bodies in 1982 (Watts et al., 1982). Furthermore, several studies have revealed the high potency of bio-inspired NMs for the effective removal of tetracycline (TC), one of the most commonly used antibiotics (Yi et al., 2018, 2019; Gopal et al., 2020).

Reductive detoxification using zero-valent metals is an important research area for the development of novel remediation technologies. Zero-valent iron (ZVI) NPs are composed of a core of zero-valent iron and a shell containing iron oxides and hydroxides, including magnetite Fe(III)2O4, goethite α-FeO(OH), and wustite (FeO). The oxide layer allows the transfer of electrons from the Fe0 core to the particle surface, where contaminants can be adsorbed on the oxide shell of the particle (Tosco et al., 2014). However, the reduction of aromatic compounds by monometallic ZVI NPs is limited under ambient temperatures and pressures. Coating ZVI NPs with noble metals such as Pd, Ni, Pt, or Cu can overcome this limitation (Stefaniuk et al., 2016). To overcome some of these challenges, bimetallic spheroid ZVI/Cu NPs (50 nm) were synthesized using pomegranate peel rind extract with a high phenolic content (Gopal et al., 2020). These were used in a batch process for TC removal, monitored using UV/Vis, redox oxidation potential, and total organic carbon spectral analyses. A TC removal efficiency of 72 ± 0.5% was obtained at an initial TC concentration of 10 mg/L at pH 7 using 750 mg/L of ZVI-Cu NPs. However, to improve the poor colloidal stability and further increase TC removal, the bimetallic NPs were supported in situ over bentonite, which has a high mechanical stability, chemical inertness, unique structural properties, and a large surface area, making it an appropriate substrate for the removal of both inorganic and organic contaminants (Soliemanzadeh and Fekri, 2017). At pH 7, the bentonite-supported nanocomposite exhibited a remarkable improvement in TC removal efficacy, at 95 ± 0.05%, and a significant decrease in nanocomposite loading, at only 150 mg/L. The adsorption of TC could be attributed to π–π interactions and cation–π bonding.

Fe3O4/Au nanocomposite derived from Carum carvi aqueous seed extract was used in a study to degrade imipenem and imatinib drugs under UV and visible light irradiation (Mirsadeghi et al., 2020). Degradation efficiencies of approximately 92% and 96% were obtained for imatinib and imipenem, respectively, with an initial concentration of 10 mg/L for both drugs, after exposure to UV light for 1200 s, whereas after exposure to visible light, the degradation efficiencies were approximately 82% and 84%, respectively. Trapping experiments were conducted using reactive species to explore the possible degradation mechanism. Three typical scavengers, triethanolamine (TEA), 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ), and isopropanol (IPA), acted as scavengers for H+, O2 •‒, and •OH, respectively. The photodegradation of imatinib was inhibited in the presence of BQ and TEA, but only a slight decline occurred when IPA was added, indicating that the photocatalytic degradation of imatinib was mainly controlled by H+ rather than •OH (Yuan et al., 2019). However, radical trapping experiments revealed that •OH had a minor effect on imatinib (Hong et al., 2016). Following exposure to visible light, the peak intensity of •OH slightly increased, indicating the presence of photocatalytic holes. The Fe3O4/Au nanocomposite stimulated electron-hole separation via the transfer of electron-holes from the valence band to the conduction band. The high oxidative potency of the holes stimulated the direct and indirect oxidation of the drugs, facilitating their degradation. During the indirect oxidation of contaminants, electron-holes in the valence band generated highly reactive •OH by reacting with H2O and OH−.

Nickel-iron nanocomposite-immobilized alginate beads were synthesized in a study using polyphenol-rich Punica granatum peel extract (K.V.G. et al., 2020). The TC removal efficiency using the green nanocomposite was 99% in a batch reactor under optimal conditions, including an initial TC concentration of 20 mg/L, a nickel-iron nanocomposite concentration of 1000 mg/L, bead weight of 20% (W/V), and a reaction time of 90 min. The maximum removal potential achieved in a column reactor was 487 ± 6.84 mg/g under a bed height of 15 cm, a TC initial concentration of 20 mg/L, and a flow rate of 1 mL/min. The TC removal mechanism was a combination of adsorption and degradation processes. In another study, three different aqueous peel extracts (i.e., Cucumis sativus, Citrus limon, and Vitis vinifera) were used to tailor the biogenic synthesis of three different types of Fe3O4 NPs that were deployed for the treatment of a mixture containing seven antibiotics (TC, tazobactam, piperacillin, trimethoprim, ampicillin, sulfamethoxazole, and erythromycin) in an aqueous solution (Stan et al., 2017). The Fe3O4 NP sample derived from the C. sativus peel extract exhibited the highest potency for the removal of the tested antibiotics, followed by the Fe3O4 NP samples derived from C. limon and V. vinifera peel extracts, which could be attributed to the differences in the biochemical constituents of each agro-biowaste extract. The adsorption of the tested antibiotics onto the synthesized Fe3O4 NPs followed the Langmuir isotherm and pseudo-second-order kinetics model.

Academic institutions propose several game-changing innovations (GCIs); however, such proposals sometimes do not reach commercialization. Researchers are frequently discouraged from pursuing entrepreneurial opportunities for their GCIs due to the effort needed to persuade investors to fund their GCIs beyond the fourth technology readiness level (TRL) (Beims et al., 2019). It is pivotal to secure funding from private investment, such as venture capitalists (VCs) to advance the innovations beyond the TRL 4 stage (Upadhyayula et al., 2018). However, most of the innovators struggle to attract investors' attention for several reasons, involving absence of motivation and marketing opportunities. Moreover, several VCs are only interested in the innovations that can reach TRL 5–6 stages to make rapid progress and yield a high return on investment. A recent review proposed a four-pronged approach for developing sustainability performance metrics that early investors can use to understand the GCIs' commercialization prospects, involving (i) conduction of a screening-level life cycle assessment (LCA) of the GCI while reducing uncertainties of underlying data and technological readiness; (ii) comparison of the LCA performance of the GCI with that of comparable commercial products in the target market; (iii) incorporation of the LCA findings into investment evaluation methods; and (iv) transformation of LCA results into indicators to reflect sustainability performance (Upadhyayula et al., 2018).

Inorganic contaminants involve toxic heavy metals and nutrient salts such as dissolved cations and anions (Lim and Foo, 2021). Although dissolved salts are natural constituents of water, the increased levels that result from anthropogenic activities can render water bodies unsuitable for human consumption in addition to affecting the aquatic fauna and flora.

Aquatic environments are primarily contaminated by heavy metals as a result of several human activities (e.g., agriculture, combustion, sewage, smelting, corroded underground pipes, and industrial plants). Conventional methods for detecting and monitoring trace metals in water bodies include but are not limited to spectroscopic techniques such as inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrophotometry, inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrophotometry, flame atomic absorption spectrophotometry, graphite furnace atomic absorption, and X-ray fluorescence spectrophotometry (Munonde and Nomngongo, 2021). However, these techniques require sample collection, pretreatment, high energy and pure gas input, infrastructural facilities, and skilled engineers and technicians to operate the instruments (Munonde and Nomngongo, 2021). Electroanalytical devices, particularly electrochemical sensors, are promising alternatives for the cost-effective, sensitive, and selective detection of heavy metals in water bodies (Munir et al., 2019; Kokab et al., 2020). To meet quality standards while also being efficient and cost-effective, four factors should be considered during wastewater treatment, involving (i) the flexibility and efficiency of the treatment methodology, (ii) reusability of treatment agents, (iii) environmental friendliness and protection, and (iv) cost-effectiveness of the used materials and applied methods (Liu et al., 2021c). Wastewater treatment technologies must not only be environmentally beneficial but also economically feasible/profitable to be key enabling. Closing loops also imposes technical and financial limitations that are primarily based on the re-use and recyclability potentials of waste streams and their byproducts (Escobar and Laibach, 2021). Moreover, effective implementation of cradle-to-cradle systems necessitates coordination between industrial sectors and waste treatment to establish profitable and interconnected networks. In this regard, industrial symbiosis can enhance technical viability while potentially decreasing transportation and waste treatment costs (Escobar and Laibach, 2021).

The unique properties of bioinspired NMs such as the high surface-area-to-volume ratio, biocompatibility, and modifiability, have sparked research interest for heavy metal detoxification in fresh water and wastewater. However, colloidal NPs tend to aggregate due to van der Waals and electrostatic interactions, which affect their stability, mobility, and reactivity (Gambinossi et al., 2015). The aggregation tendency of NPs can be reduced via surface modifications and coatings via the attachment of inorganic shells and/or organic molecules, which stabilize NPs and prevent oxidation, thereby providing specific functionalities that can be selective for metal ion uptake during wastewater treatment (Saravanan et al., 2021). This uptake of heavy metals by NMs can be accomplished through surface-site binding, selective adsorption, electrostatic interactions, and a modified ligand combination (Gupta et al., 2021).

The separation and recovery of NMs from treated water are technical bottlenecks. Magnetic IO nanosorbents can be recovered by applying an external magnetic field. Nanosized magnetite (Fe3O4) NPs have a high adsorption capacity for several contaminants owing to their large surface area and highly reactive surface sites. Semicrystalline Fe3O4 NPs were synthesized using coconut husk extract at room temperature (Sebastian et al., 2018). Phenolics in the husk extract mediated the formation of Fe3O4 NPs, which were effective at adsorbing Ca and Cd ions from an aqueous solution with a maximum adsorption potential of 13.7 mg/g and 9.6 mg/g, respectively, at a pH of 6 and a temperature of 30 °C. Temperatures higher than 30 °C cleaved the bonds between the active sites of the adsorbent and the metal ions, leading to inhibited electrostatic interactions between the active sites of the Fe3O4 NPs and the metal ions due to the increase in metal ion kinetic energy as well as the rotational or vibrational energy of the active adsorbent sites (Anirudhan and Suchithra, 2010). The adsorption capacity of magnetic Fe3O4 NPs (50–200 nm) synthesized via chemical coprecipitation using sugarcane bagasse was also investigated in another study (Jabasingh et al., 2018). The green synthesized Fe3O4 NPs showed superparamagnetic properties under an external magnetic field at room temperature with a saturation magnetization value of 9.192 emu/g. The prepared Fe3O4 NPs (at a concentration of 800 mg/150 mL) exhibited the highest Cr6+ removal capacity over a reaction time of 180 min with a pH 5 and a temperature of 318 K. The sorption efficiency for Cr6+ removal by the regenerated Fe3O4 NPs was nearly as high as that of newly synthesized Fe3O4 NPs over eight successive regeneration cycles with removal efficiencies of 91.13%, 91.11%, 91.09%, 91.05%, 91.04%, 91.02%, 91.01%, and 89.98%. However, the sorption capacity decreased by almost 2 and 3% from the eighth to the twelfth cycles and from the twelfth to the twentieth cycles, respectively, suggesting the decreased sorption of Cr6+ due to complexation and electric attraction sorption (Fig. 5 ). In another study, the maximum adsorption capacity of tea waste extract-derived Fe3O4 NPs (∼5–25 nm) was 188.69 and 153.8 mg/g for As (III) and As (V), respectively (Lunge et al., 2014). In economic terms, the cost of tea waste derived Fe3O4 NPs was calculated based on the raw material and process costs. The cost of Fe3O4 NPs was 135.34 R/kg (rupees per kilogram), while the cost of removing As (III) from the water was 0.14 R/L.

The particle diameter of green synthesized IONPs was reduced from 200 to 50 nm by increasing the concentration of tangerine peel extract (Ehrampoush et al., 2015). The highest adsorption capacity of Cd (II) was 90% at a pH of 4 with an adsorbent dose of 0.4 g/100 mL. Cd (II) adsorption by IONPs followed the Freundlich adsorption model, indicating multilayer adsorption. The reaction followed pseudo-second-order kinetics, indicating chemical interaction between the FeNPs and Cd (II). Citrus maxima peel-derived carbon NPs acted as a sensitive and selective probe for the detection of Hg2+ (i.e., LOD as low as 0.23 nM) (Lu et al., 2012). The negative health effects of zinc overexposure urged the United States Environmental Regulatory Agency and the United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization to set limits for Zn in drinking water (5 mg/L) (Jain et al., 2015). Bio-inspired SeNPs (160–180 nm) were synthesized in a study via the incubation of Se with anaerobic granular sludge for the sequestration of Zn (II) in wastewater (Jain et al., 2015). The reaction time and pH were the most influential parameters affecting Zn (II) adsorption by the SeNPs and 70% of the Zn (II) was adsorbed within a few minutes with the maximum removal achieved after 4 h. SeNPs effectively sequestered Zn (II) over a wide pH range (3.9–6.5), which could be useful for treating wastewater generated from the electroplating industry and acid mine drainage. At an initial pH ranging from 2.9 to 3.8, the adsorption of Zn2+ by SeNPs followed a ligand-like adsorption mechanism (type II), through which ligands bound to solid surfaces by replacing OH− and reducing the repulsion between solid particles and cations, thereby facilitating cation binding either at the same site as the ligand or at other sites. Additionally, ligands maintained electro-neutrality on the adsorbent surface by increasing the number of active sites involved in adsorption.

Water pollution by biological contaminants such as pathogenic bacteria, fungi, viruses, and protozoa, causes the spread of infectious diseases such as cholera, typhoid, and amoebic dysentery (Srivastav et al., 2020). Approximately 1.8 million annual deaths are due to water-borne infections worldwide (Zheng et al., 2018). Water-borne pathogenic microorganisms can be eliminated through disinfection, the final but most important step in water treatment (Huang et al., 2020). A model disinfection method should have specific characteristics, including (i) a broad antimicrobial spectrum over a short period; (ii) no hazardous intermediates; (iii) low/no toxicity; (iv) low energy utilization; (v) simple handling; (vi) ease of storage; (vii) safe disposal; and (vii) non-corrosiveness (Zhang et al., 2016b). Large-scale water disinfection can be accomplished using chemicals, UV radiation, or a combination of both (Huang et al., 2020). Chlorine, monochloramines, chlorine dioxide, bromine, and ozone are among the chemicals that can be used for water disinfection (Tang et al., 2020). Chlorine is widely used for water disinfection because of its potency for inhibiting pathogenic microorganisms at a low-cost (Mazhar et al., 2020). However, chlorine-based chemicals release disinfection byproducts (DBPs) during disinfection processes, which are long-lasting harmful chlorinated compounds released due to reactions between free chlorine species and naturally occurring organic matter that may be carcinogenic, depending on their chemical behavior (Henson et al., 2014). In addition to producing DBPs at high concentrations, chlorination is ineffective against some resistant microbes (Grellier et al., 2015). Ozone-based disinfection is relatively expensive and bromate (i.e., a harmful DBP) can be generated in the presence of bromide ions (Srivastav et al., 2020). Agro-industrial biowaste-derived NMs such as TiO2 NPs (Abu-Dalo et al., 2019) and AgNPs (Mehwish et al., 2021) have shown excellent performance in water disinfection applications against pathogenic water-borne bacteria such as Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Staphylococcus aureus, Salmonella enterica, Escherichia coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae, and Vibrio cholera. Several NMs, including chitosan NPs, AgNPs, TiO2 NPs, and carbon-based NMs exhibit strong antimicrobial potential as they damage the cell membrane via the release of toxic metal ions, direct contact, and ROS generation. The antibacterial mechanisms are shown in (Fig. 6 ). Unlike traditional chemical disinfectants, green synthesized NMs inactivate microorganisms without releasing harmful DBPs.

Water supply and treatment systems should incorporate technologies that consider the water-energy-food-ecosystem nexus during urban and rural planning (Cipolletta et al., 2021). In this context, the implementation of technologies that can preserve environmental resources, territorial, and lands is a major issue. Waste sorting policies that reduce the generation of municipal solid waste, while boosting resource recycling are becoming common (Llanquileo-Melgarejo et al., 2021). Sewage sludge (SS) is produced on a large scale as a byproduct from wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs), containing large amounts of organic matter and other pollutants that require proper treatment, management, and disposal to fulfill the concepts of reducing, reusing, and recycling (Yang et al., 2015; Fijalkowski et al., 2017). Furthermore, costs of SS handling and disposal comprise approximately half of the total costs of wastewater treatment. As a result, its reuse is environmentally sustainable and economically feasible (Ghafarzadeh et al., 2017; Capodaglio and Callegari, 2018). Studies based on geographical information systems (GISs) have been implemented in several disciplines as decision-making tools because they can provide optimal logistical solutions related to location-allocation-based issues (Comber et al., 2015; Laasasenaho et al., 2019). Furthermore, GIS-based spatial models can provide information to assist in the optimization of supply chains and they are useful tools for local stakeholders and policymakers in identifying appropriate regions for optimal logistic solutions and minimizing transportation costs (Valenti, 2021). In one study, a GIS-based model was established and applied to the Emilia-Romagna region to study the existing WWTP networks and biogas systems to valorize SS for bioenergy production (i.e., biomethane) and minimize any adverse environmental impacts (Valenti, 2021). The results of the GIS analyses facilitated the localization of the highest productive territorial areas and highlighted where SS are abundantly located to be exploited in agricultural biogas plants. The obtained findings could aid in the planning of appropriate policy interventions and the development of a regulatory framework that could have a significant role in the establishment of a more sustainable biomethane sector in line with the green economy goals.

The European initiatives on the circular economy emphasize the importance of the circular and sustainable management of waste materials (Smol, 2021). A recent study showed that the estimated quantity of agricultural wastes, co-products, and by-products (AWCB) in the period between 2010 and 2016 was approximately 18.4 billion tonnes in the European Union (EU) (Bedoić et al., 2019). The percentages were ∼44%, ∼31%, ∼22%, and ∼2% for the vegetable, animal, cereal, and fruit sectors, respectively. Moreover, this study showed that the dispersion of the quantities of AWCB was mainly dependent on climate conditions, land activities, and human consumption. Countries with less available land areas, large industrial zones, and with high population density were the biggest producers of the AWCB in the animal and vegetable sectors, including Belgium, France, Ireland, Germany, and the Netherlands. Consequently, Western European countries have a high opportunity to use co- and by-products generated from animal farming and vegetable cultivation activities. South European countries, on the other hand, with large land areas and mild weather conditions, dominated in terms of the amount of AWCB generated in the fruit sector. Hence, making use of citrus co- and byproducts in these countries should be studied in greater depth in future studies. The high quantities of the AWCB of the cereal sector were observed in Central and Eastern European countries, including France and Germany.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioactive molecules involved in the chelation and formulation of green NMs

ce:section.ce:para: Flavonoids are polyphenolic compounds that can chelate metal ions and reduce them to form nanostructures (El-Seedi et al., 2019). The reducing potency of flavonoids is attributed to the abundance of key functional groups such as hydroxyl and carbonyl moieties (Marslin et al., 2018). Flavonoids play a key role during the early stages of NP formation (i.e., nucleation) and the subsequent aggregation (Makarov et al., 2014). The tautomeric transformation from the enol-to keto-form releases reactive hydrogen atoms, which reduce metal ions. Another possible mechanism is their ability to chelate metal ions via the π-electrons in their carbonyl functional groups (Makarov et al., 2014). For instance, quercetin is a flavonoid with strong chelating properties due to its ability to chelate at multiple sites, including the carbonyl and hydroxyl groups at the C3 and C5 positions and the catechol group at sites C3′ and C4’. Under alkaline conditions, diosmin and hesperetin (flavonoid molecules) were isolated from Citrus peels to mediate the synthesis of a stable colloidal suspension of spherical-shaped AuNPs with a size of 10 nm (Sierra et al., 2016). According to computational simulations, hydroxyl groups played a significant role in the reduction of Au+3 cations. The charged hesperetin species strongly interacted with the Au+3 cations, causing a net charge transfer and electrochemical reduction of the Au+3 cations.

Phenolic acids are phenolic derivatives that contain a phenolic ring and at least one carboxylic functional group and can be classified as cinnamic or benzoic acid derivatives based on the number of hydroxylation sites on aromatic rings. Under alkaline conditions, phenolic acids tend to exhibit a high oxidation potential, which can initiate NP nucleation (Aslam et al., 2021). Caffeic acid (CA) is distinguished by its strong adsorption potential, primarily attributed to the catecholic functional groups with good metal sorbing moieties (Amini and Akbari, 2019). In one study, the oxidation of caffeic acid hydroxyl functional groups provided electrons for the neutralization of Au3+ (Kim et al., 2016), while in another study, density-functional theory (DFT) calculations revealed that the adsorption of CA onto ZnO NPs and TiO2 NPs was induced by a bridging bidentate bond configuration (Zhang et al., 2015). The adsorption energetics and configurations of CA and its deprotonated forms on the Miller index Au surfaces were influenced by surface coverage and adsorbate type. Adjustments in the adsorption properties and surface energies prompted the synthesis of AuNPs with various morphologies.

Monosaccharides with an aldehyde group, such as glucose, can act as reductants, whereas fructose (with a keto group) can act as an antioxidant via a series of tautomeric transformation reactions from the ketone to aldehyde forms (Liu et al., 2021d). It is worth noting that glucose is a stronger reductant than fructose. However, the reducing potential of disaccharide and polysaccharide chains is highly dependent on the ability of their monosaccharide constituents to form open chains that allow metal ions to chelate with an aldehyde group. The two disaccharide molecules, maltose and lactose, can act as reducers because their monomers can provide an open-chain form. Meanwhile, sucrose is a non-reducing sugar (Panigrahi et al., 2004). Polysaccharides can be categorized into cationic, anionic, and neutral types such as chitosan, alginate, and dextran, respectively, and can mediate the synthesis of NPs through covalent or ionic cross-linking with various functional groups such as amino, carboxylic, and hydroxyl groups (Mizrahy and Peer, 2012).

Essential oils are complex structures composed of volatile organic compounds that plants produce as secondary metabolites (Mashwani et al., 2016). Essential oils include terpenes, terpenoids, sesquiterpenes, and oxygenated molecules such as alcohols, esters, ethers, aldehydes, ketones, lactones, phenols, and phenol ethers (Edris, 2007). Terpenes are the most common naturally occurring essential oil class and consist of five-carbon isoprene units that can be assembled and modified in various ways. Terpenoids are oxygenated terpenes that account for 90% of plant essential oils (Gómez-López et al., 2020). Terpenoids are composed of simple hydrocarbon molecules called isoprenoids that combine to produce monoterpenoids and sesquiterpenoids. Terpenoids provide an organic matrix that can coat NPs, preventing agglomeration and providing long-term stability (Mashwani et al., 2016). For instance, the terpenoid-based synthesis of AgNPs occurs via a two-step reaction process, involving the reduction of Ag ions, followed by capping and agglomeration to form oligomeric clusters of AgNPs (Fig. S2), supplementary materials (Mashwani et al., 2016).

Proteins are naturally occurring polymeric molecules composed of amino acids and confer unique properties such as high stability, low toxicity, biodegradability, and binding affinity to NPs (Vuignier et al., 2010). Proteins such as elastin (Han et al., 2015), albumin (Tarhini et al., 2018), collagen (Nogueira et al., 2019), and silk (Sangappa et al., 2019) can mediate the green synthesis of NMs. Amino acids have demonstrated a high potential for NM synthesis since the 2000s (de Matos and Courrol, 2017). Metal NP formation by short peptides (i.e., amino acids) is primarily based on two factors; (i) the amino acid reduction potential and (ii) the amino acid capping (binding) potential. Amino acids bind to metal ions via the amino or carbonyl groups found in the main or the side chains of the amino acid. Polypeptides are composed of repeated amino acid sequences and their hydrophobic ends aid in the elastomerization and self-assembly of nanostructures (Nitta and Numata, 2013). Amino acids have varying potentials for metal ion binding and reduction to nanoscale forms. For instance, lysine, cysteine, arginine, and methionine can bind and reduce Ag+ ions to AgNPs (Giovannini et al., 2020). Other studies indicated that aspartate can reduce tetrachloroauric acid to form Au0, while lysine and valine cannot (Mandal et al., 2002). Twenty α-amino acids were evaluated for their ability to bind and reduce metal ions (Tan et al., 2010). Tryptophan exhibited the strongest reduction potential for Au+ ions, while histidine exhibited the strongest binding to Au+ ions. The reduction potency of a peptide relies on specific reducing amino acid residues, whose activity can be monitored by neighboring residues with different Au0 binding strengths. Additionally, it has been observed that the net peptide charge affects the nucleation and growth of AuNPs. Kinetic effects play a key role in controlling the shape and size of the NPs produced during peptide morpho-synthesis. A shape-directing sequence should be present in the peptide to facilitate the formation of the desired morphology. Additionally, peptides should possess a strong reducing sequence to accelerate the reduction kinetics and produce smaller Au nanoplates. A reduction rate regulator may therefore be positioned next to the reducing sequence to achieve better results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Encompassing green chemistry, sustainability, circular economy, nexus thinking, and zero-waste manufacturing
ce:section.ce:para: The book “Green Chemistry: Theory and Practice”, authored by Anastas and Warner and published in 1998, represented a preeminent breakthrough in the development of green chemistry. It prompted the reduction and elimination of the use and release of hazardous substances during the design, processing, and application of chemical products (Anastas, 1999). Green chemistry emphasizes the efficient use of renewable resources as well as the elimination/reduction of waste materials and avoiding the use of hazardous chemicals and solvents during manufacturing. The idea of green chemistry was proposed in the early 1990s and was rapidly accepted and adopted by the industries as a novel chemistry approach that opposed the “pollute-and-then-clean-up” approach, a common industrial practice (Centi and Perathoner, 2003). The twelve green chemistry principles laid the groundwork for future advances in green manufacturing. It is critical to emphasize that these principles comprise an integrated design system rather than twelve distinct goals. Since 1990, several metrics have been developed to assess the impact of green processes. The E-factor and atom economy were the first two green metrics proposed for the chemical industry and organic synthesis (Trost, 1991). Later, other metrics were developed for specific chemical disciplines; both energy- and mass-based metrics (Sheldon, 2018). Another approach is sustainable chemistry, which indicates how resources such as energy must be used at a rate that can be replaced naturally and how the generation of waste should not precede waste remediation (Horváth, 2018). However, it lacks clear and effective operationalization. Sustainable development describes a development mode that meets the current generation's needs without jeopardizing the ability of future generations to meet their own needs. The three components of sustainability are commonly referred to as the 3Ps (i.e., people, planet, and profit). In contrast to green chemistry, sustainability includes a financial aspect. The United Nations established 17 SDGs to provide global economies with the framework needed to address the major challenges confronting society and the environment (Kapoor et al., 2020). The underlying value of the 17 SDGs is based on practicing the most efficient and responsible use of resources and emphasizing the transition to a circular economy.

The circular economy is an economic approach that was developed to replace the linear economic model in an attempt to reduce, reuse, recycle, and recover materials during the manufacturing, distribution, and consumption processes to attain sustainable development (Fig. 9 a) (Kirchherr et al., 2017). The fundamental theoretical basis of the circular economy is closely associated with the academic field of “environmental economics”, which gradually developed in the 1960s in response to the growing concerns related to economic development and environmental sustainability (Massaro et al., 2021). For a successful transition to a circular economy, it is necessary to balance industrial and economic development, environmental protection, and efficient resource utilization. Since the beginning of the industrial revolution, the majority of worldwide economies have been entirely based on linear policies (take, make, and dispose of) (Fig. 9b) (Maina et al., 2017), which led to the overuse of resources, impeding economic regeneration and proper planning for the reuse of materials. International policy frameworks have started to shift away from the previous unsustainable approaches towards more environmentally friendly and resource-conserving approaches (Mendonça and Fonseca, 2018). The theoretical foundation of the circular economy is derived from concepts such as industrial green economy (Georgeson et al., 2017), cradle-to-cradle (Bach et al., 2018), biomimicry (Hayes et al., 2020), industrial ecology (Walker et al., 2020), bioeconomy (Stegmann et al., 2020), and natural capitalism (Flynn, 2021). These approaches represent different perceptions of circular economy that have emerged as academic and industrial visions to achieve the primary goals of sustainability and solve environmental and socio-economic problems (D'amato et al., 2019). In 2012, bioeconomy was defined by the European Commission as “the production of renewable biological resources and the conversion of these resources and waste streams into value-added products such as feed, food, bio-based products, and bioenergy” (Filho, 2018). The bioeconomy encompasses the zero-waste concept, which implies that resources can be transformed into valuable products and that waste streams should be utilized in a sustainable way (Islam, 2017). Three prerequisites are required for a sustainable bioeconomy, including sustainable resource bases, sustainable production/consumption processes and/or products, and circular flux of materials (Kumar et al., 2019).

Similarly, nexus thinking, which arose as part of early environmental and economic discussions, represents the integration of research and policy analysis across multiple sectors (Wichelns, 2017). This approach aims to achieve resource sustainability through three main pillars (i.e., food, energy, and water) (Fig. 10 ). The concept gained popularity at the World Economic Forum in 2008, when the global challenges of economic development were discussed from the perspective of the water–energy nexus (Waughray, 2011). The concept gained further traction following Hoff's participation at the Water, Energy, and Food Security Nexus Conference in Bonn (Hoff, 2011). Some recent studies have emphasized the innovativeness and key distinction of nexus thinking for integrated natural resource management. The primary distinction is that almost all nexus studies seek to build an interconnected system between the three elements water, energy, and food to provide a sustainable and secure future (Slorach et al., 2020). The integration of the circular economy with the nexus approach has the potential to provide a promising platform for optimizing resource utilization while simultaneously reducing waste.

Waste management represents an immense challenge for decision-makers. In this context, zero-waste manufacturing (ZWM) is a roadmap for the future of manufacturing to address the rising problems of waste accumulation. ZWM is a comprehensive waste management approach that entails a transition to a circular economy by developing technologies and systems that can eliminate waste to the greatest possible extent through material reuse and recycling (Curran and Williams, 2012). Net zero-emissions (NZEs) are critical to transition away from conventional industrial practices, by using waste from one industry as an input material for another industry.
