NZE aims to promote industrial production with a low impact on natural environmental resources and can be divided into the following goals; (i) zero-waste resources, (ii) zero-waste during manufacturing, (iii) zero emissions, (iv) zero-waste during the product shelf life, and (v) zero employment of toxic substances (Singh et al., 2017). The zero-waste manufacturing approach has been lauded by policymakers because it promotes sustainable manufacturing and consumption as well as optimal resource recycling and recovery (Zaman, 2015). The visionary zero-waste approach considers waste as a potential resource that can be used based on its value.

The implementation of the zero-waste approach is beneficial on a four-dimensional basis, involving (i) social, (ii) economic and financial, (iii) environmental, and (iv) industrial aspects. Changing waste disposal attitudes significantly reduce the public's exposure to life-threatening risks while also providing communities with diverse opportunities through the collection, recycling, and valorization of waste (Pietzsch et al., 2017). The main industrial and economic benefits include an increased profit and income flow, and reduced costs due to waste recycling and valorization. Extending the lifetime of sanitary landfills, increasing environmental protection, and effective consumption of raw waste materials can help in reducing the amount of greenhouse gases emitted, hazardous materials produced, and energy utilized (Singh et al., 2017). Industrial and stakeholder benefits include an improved performance, increased productivity, and sustainable industrial practices. Social involvement is necessary to ensure the success of zero-waste management. This can be accomplished by implementing social campaigns and programs that focus on environmental education and the proper methods for waste disposal and segregation (Xanthos and Walker, 2017). The expansion of reverse logistics and clustering of symbiotic organizations to regulate the flow of materials, energy consumption, and the transferal of waste generated from one industrial sector to another are additional activities required for achieving zero-waste manufacturing (Beitzen-Heineke et al., 2017).

Moreover, the development of clean valorization technologies by academic researchers is essential and must be adequately distributed and circulated among the prominent industrial sectors. In light of the above, the success of this approach depends on governance, planning, and operationalization. Successful zero-waste management requires a set of distinct criteria that include socio-cultural behavior, environmental and economic aspects, governance, and policy (Zaman, 2014). In brief, success is closely related to the economic, social, and political actions of a society.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Economic, environmental, and health impacts of valorizing agro-industrial biowaste to green NMs

ce:section.ce:para:
One of the key points that progresses economic/business models is the reduction of capital costs, which can be accomplished by (i) manufacturing valuable products with zero-cost materials, or (ii) employing zero-waste manufacturing procedures that prevent additional expenses that are associated with waste disposal. The valorization of biowastes can promote industrial symbiosis by repurposing the output of one industry for use as an input in another (Baldassarre et al., 2019). This can help industries reduce the amount of accumulated biowaste while improving their public image among customers and business partners (Barros et al., 2021). Designing a circular product can benefit the supply chain, logistics, and manufacturing processes by closing the material and energy consumption cycles. When resources and/or products end up in a circular system, better strategic planning can be achieved in terms of costs. The application of green technologies, renewable energies, and the transition from a linear to a circular economy can boost industry and business competitiveness, incomes, job creation, and innovation. When planning to valorize agro-industrial biowaste, various criteria need to be carefully considered from an industrial viewpoint, including:
(i)
Segregation and proper source layout of feedstocks for factories,
(ii)
Estimation of product yields based on the organic composition of biomass,
(iii)
Evaluation of the recovery procedures to achieve large-scale production while avoiding potentially environmentally hazardous pathways,
(iv)
Increasing the technical knowledge of stakeholders and industrial personnel to invest in agro-industrial biowaste valorization. Companies that adopt more circular practices will be able to reduce tangible costs, such as material usage and waste disposal through resource recovery initiatives, as well as intangible costs such as the potential negative (or lower) reputation of companies that neglect sustainable practices.

Applying circular economy-based approaches to valorize agro-industrial biowaste for the production of green NMs would help achieve multiple goals such as biowaste reduction, upcycling to high-value-added products, and environmental protection (Omran et al., 2018). As previously stated, significant efforts have been made to develop environmentally friendly procedures for the green assembly of NMs and to advance the field of nanobiotechnology. Despite the successful chemical and physical fabrication of well-designed NMs, the synthesis methodologies are too harsh, complicated, non-eco-friendly, and expensive. The valorization of agro-industrial biowaste to biologically fabricated nanostructures provides a distinct platform for replacing the chemically and physically synthesized NMs. This substitution is a critical step for protecting the environment from the toxic chemicals used in the chemical synthesis of NMs and preventing greenhouse gas emissions if such biowastes need to be treated via traditional methods (landfilling or incineration). The environmental impacts are distinguishable throughout a product's life cycle, including its production, utilization, and disposal. Currently, the advancement of Industry 4.0 in conjunction with digital transformation are promoting the preservation of materials/energy, hence achieving environmental sustainability (Oláh et al., 2020).

Despite the previously mentioned positive environmental impacts of valorizing agro-industrial biowaste to green bio-nanosorbents, bio-nanocatalysts, and bio-nanodisinfectants, it is critical to analyze, assess, and comprehend the environmental and health risks associated with the use of such NMs. Nanotoxicology is now considered an important field for assessing bio-nano interactions (Tarrahi et al., 2021). Although the properties of green NMs are comparable to those of chemically and physically synthesized NMs, nanotoxicological studies can determine whether and to what extent these properties may endanger the environment and organisms (Hu et al., 2016). The three significant factors determining the toxicity of NMs upon their association with living cells are durability, dose, and dimension, or the “3Ds”. The physicochemical properties of NMs such as their size, morphology, stability, aggregation, surface chemistry, and particle mass affect their toxicity. Some environmental factors can also affect NM toxicity, including pH, temperature, ionic strength, UV light, and the presence of electrolytes and other contaminants (Jahan et al., 2017). For safe water and wastewater treatment applications, the toxicity of green synthesized NMs to aquatic plants, vertebrate models, and algae must be investigated using appropriate tools and tailored assays. In this context, specialized methods such as single-particle inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, single-particle inductively coupled plasma time-of-flight mass spectrometry, and automated electron microscopy have been developed (Neubauer et al., 2017). However, due to the limitations of the currently available analytical methods, innovative approaches are required to analyze NMs in complex environmental media.

The principle of “reduction, refinement, and replacement” (3Rs) is currently used as an alternative to in vivo animal testing to avoid unethical procedures and overcome the drawbacks associated with animal experiments (Huang et al., 2021). Non-mammalian alternative models may be better alternatives to deal with the ethical concerns related to the animal models used for assessing NM safety. Other non-animal approaches involve in vitro and in silico approaches, which can meet the 3Rs principle. In vitro nanotoxicity assays involve tissue engineering and cell culturing, whereas in silico models are dependent on prediction via molecular docking, molecular dynamic simulations, and quantitative structure-activity relationship modeling. Advances in artificial intelligence and machine learning have prompted the development of innovative approaches for toxicity assessments. Using specific models such as the quantitative structure–activity relationship (QSAR) and computational toxicology models allows the factors responsible for toxic interactions to be clarified (Fig. S3), supplementary materials (Saini and Srivastava, 2018). Such models must account for all possible interactions that a substance may encounter to produce accurate results. In nanotechnology, computational modeling is used to establish a link between NM biokinetics, the dynamics of biological responses, and the fate of NMs once they reach the target organ of interest (García-Quintero and Palencia, 2021). As a result, in silico approaches allow systematic simulations for assessing the potential risks of NMs. Currently, nano-specific databases such as the Online Chemical Modeling Environment (https://ochem.eu/home/show.do), NanoDatabank (https://nanohub.org/projects/nanodatabank), NanoHub (https://nanohub.org), NanoMILE (http://www.nanomile.eu-vri.eu), and ModNanoTox (https://www.birmingham.ac.uk/generic/modnanotox/index.aspx) are employed for assessing the risks of NMs (Pikula et al., 2020). The major drawback of in vitro methods is the failure to completely mimic in vivo conditions. For in silico computational approaches, the current computational models used to assess nanotoxicity still have significant obstacles and drawbacks. This is due to variations in the size, surface properties, aggregation tendency, and dispersion of NMs in vivo, significantly influencing their potential toxicity (Ribeiro et al., 2017). For these reasons animal models are still the standard for clarifying the toxicity associated with new substances.

In recent decades, nanotechnology-based approaches for water and wastewater purification and treatment have gained widespread acceptance among researchers and industrial manufacturers. Green NMs synthesized via the one-pot valorization of zero-cost, biodegradable, and readily available agro-industrial biowaste were emphasized in this review. According to the reviewed literature, a wide range of green bio-nanosorbents and bio-nanocatalysts have been tested for the removal of organic (e.g., dyes, phenols, nitrophenols, chlorinated compounds, organophosphorus pesticides, and pharmaceutical compounds) and inorganic (e.g., heavy metals) water contaminants. Biologically fabricated NMs can adsorb water pollutants and/or catalyze oxidation and reduction degradation reactions, converting organic pollutants to non-toxic forms. Moreover, some bio-synthesized NMs act as effective bio-nanodisinfectants against water-borne pathogenic microbes. Thus, the green synthesis of NMs using agro-industrial biowaste is a cost-effective, energy-efficient, and environmentally acceptable approach that can significantly enhance wastewater treatment. Some encouraging findings include the ability of bio-inspired nanocatalysts and nanosorbents to function over wide temperature and pH ranges and the achievement of high recyclability without a significant loss in their adsorptive or catalytic potential. Some even exhibited high catalytic potential in a very short reaction time. In addition, biologically developed NMs can remove several types of water contaminants. Several bioactive molecules such as polyphenols, monosaccharides, disaccharides, polysaccharides, terpenoids, proteins, polypeptides, and amino acids can mediate the green formulation, reduction, capping, and stabilization of NMs. The valorization of agro-industrial biowaste adheres to the fundamental principles of green chemistry, sustainability, circular economy, nexus thinking, and zero-waste manufacturing. This review thus emphasizes how the valorization of agro-industrial biowaste achieves the twin goals of resource upcycling and waste minimization, producing high-value products while protecting the environment from the negative impacts of biowaste accumulation.

An integrated approach is proposed to help mapping the barriers and opportunities for effective valorization of agro-industrial biowastes and some action items in this regard are as follows:
(i)
It is critical to promote a sustainable and integrated waste management framework based on the “reduce, reuse, and recycle” concept by identifying the major waste streams and the various stages of the waste management cycle, including the collection, transportation, source separation, treatment, recovery, and disposal of biowaste.
(ii)
Comprehensive and integrated assessment tools such as the sustainability index based on environmental, social, and economic analyses are required for the development of long-term policy frameworks.
(iii)
Stakeholders should take advantage of the breakthroughs in data analysis to facilitate more reliable data interpretation and provide insights to develop new strategies and regulations. Political regulations and the empowerment of stakeholders can help address the challenges and lack of clarity and raise awareness and social consciousness about the profitable opportunities behind the valorization of agro-industrial biowaste into valuable products.
(iv)
Crop machinery for the efficient collection, segregation, and transportation of agro-industrial biowaste should be developed through R&D.
(v)
Reliable and comprehensive data describing the current waste situation must be precisely prepared.
(vi)
Cost-benefit analyses and techno-commercial feasibility studies are required.

Certain criteria should be considered when using green NMs synthesized via the valorization of agro-industrial biowaste for wastewater treatment:
(i)
There is a knowledge gap because most studies focus on the treatment of a few specific types of water pollutants, primarily dyes and phenolic compounds, with little attention paid to other water contaminants.
(ii)
The vast majority of assessments and evaluations have been conducted on simulated samples with only a few field trials investigating real industrial effluents.
(iii)
In recent years, remarkable progress has been made in the fields of supercomputing and computational modeling. Various approaches such as DFT, molecular dynamics simulations, and docking studies can be used to understand and study the mechanistic details of possible interactions between biomolecules and green synthesized NMs.
(iv)
Before commercializing any new agro-industrial biowaste-derived NMs, the effects on human health should be thoroughly evaluated and assessed. The potential permeation of green NMs to humans is primarily determined by the size, shape, and NP charge owing to the morphology, chemical configuration, extent of agglomeration, and hydrophilicity. Nonetheless, cytotoxic studies on bio-nanocatalysts, bio-nanosorbents, and bio-nanodisinfectants are currently lacking.
(v)
LCA is a powerful tool to thoroughly investigate the potential environmental impacts such as ecological influences, resource consumption, and human health. Implementing LCA studies can help select and evaluate the best raw materials, synthetic techniques, and product yields.
Collection of data, B.O.; Writing-original draft preparation, B.O.; Review and editing, K.-H.B. All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.
This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2021R1F1A1060297).
